perturbation.original_text: 2. Obligations of Receiving Party. The receiving party will protect Confidential Information by using at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of such Confidential Information as the receiving party uses to protect its own confidential or proprietary information. The receiving party will neither disclose nor copy Confidential Disclosure except as necessary for its employees, agents or representatives with a need to know, provided that any such employee, agent or representative shall have agreed in writing, as a condition to his or her employment or engagement or in order to obtain Confidential Information, to be bound by non-disclosure and non-use obligations substantially similar to this Agreement. Any copies which are made will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and shall be reproduced with the disclosing party's proprietary rights notices in the same manner in which such notices appear in the original copy provided by the disclosing party. The receiving party will not use the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate a business relationship with the disclosing party or otherwise in pursuance of such a relationship. Nothing in this Agreement will be construed to limit the receiving party's right to independently develop information similar to the Confidential Information or to compete with the disclosing party so long as the receiving party does not violate its obligations hereunder regarding the non-disclosure and use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Obligations of Receiving Party. The receiving party may, at its sole discretion, protect Confidential Information. The receiving party will neither disclose nor copy Confidential Disclosure except as necessary for its employees, agents or representatives with a need to know, provided that any such employee, agent or representative shall have agreed in writing, as a condition to his or her employment or engagement or in order to obtain Confidential Information, to be bound by non-disclosure and non-use obligations substantially similar to this Agreement. Any copies which are made will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and shall be reproduced with the disclosing party's proprietary rights notices in the same manner in which such notices appear in the original copy provided by the disclosing party. The receiving party will not use the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate a business relationship with the disclosing party or otherwise in pursuance of such a relationship. Nothing in this Agreement will be construed to limit the receiving party's right to independently develop information similar to the Confidential Information or to compete with the disclosing party so long as the receiving party does not violate its obligations hereunder regarding the non-disclosure and use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The altered text in Section 2 replaces the mandatory language about protecting Confidential Information (the receiving party 'will protect') with discretionary language (the receiving party 'may, at its sole discretion, protect'). This creates an in-text contradiction because other parts of the agreement, such as the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 1 and the remedies outlined in Section 6, still assume the receiving party has a definite obligation to protect the information. This undermines the entire premise of the NDA, as it makes the protection of confidential information optional, directly contradicting the expectations set by the rest of the agreement. The change contradicts the original intention of the NDA, where the receiving party has a duty to protect the confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Remedies. The receiving party acknowledges that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement, and the disclosing party will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies will not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement but will be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the disclosing party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 2 alters the Receiving Party's obligation to protect Confidential Information from 'will protect' to 'may, at its sole discretion, protect'. This directly contradicts Section 6, which assumes a guaranteed obligation and allows the disclosing party to seek remedies for 'any breach of this Agreement'. If the Receiving Party has sole discretion on whether to protect the information, it is not a guaranteed obligation, therefore there is no 'breach of agreement' if they don't protect it, making Section 6 unenforceable. This meets the definition of inconsistencies, as Section 2 and Section 6 provide conflicting obligations, creating an in-text contradiction regarding which terms should be enforced.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will neither disclose nor copy Confidential Disclosure except as necessary for its employees, agents or representatives with a need to know, provided that any such employee, agent or representative shall have agreed in writing, as a condition to his or her employment or engagement or in order to obtain Confidential Information, to be bound by non-disclosure and non-use obligations substantially similar to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will neither disclose nor copy Confidential Disclosure, even if it is necessary for its employees, agents or representatives with a need to know. No employee, agent or representative needs to agree in writing, as a condition to his or her employment or engagement or in order to obtain Confidential Information, to be bound by non-disclosure and non-use obligations substantially similar to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction by directly negating the exception for disclosure to employees, agents, or representatives with a 'need to know' who have agreed to confidentiality. The original clause allows for disclosure under specific conditions. The changed clause prohibits it entirely, even under those conditions. This contradicts the implied allowance for internal disclosure within the receiving party's organization that was previously provided for, and negates the previous condition of employees needing to agree to non-disclosure agreements. This change creates a direct contradiction in section 2 about the terms of how confidentiality shall be enforced.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Obligations of Receiving Party. The receiving party may, at its sole discretion, protect Confidential Information. The receiving party will neither disclose nor copy Confidential Disclosure, even if it is necessary for its employees, agents or representatives with a need to know. No employee, agent or representative needs to agree in writing, as a condition to his or her employment or engagement or in order to obtain Confidential Information, to be bound by non-disclosure and non-use obligations substantially similar to this Agreement. Any copies which are made will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and shall be reproduced with the disclosing party's proprietary rights notices in the same manner in which such notices appear in the original copy provided by the disclosing party. The receiving party will not use the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate a business relationship with the disclosing party or otherwise in pursuance of such a relationship. Nothing in this Agreement will be construed to limit the receiving party's right to independently develop information similar to the Confidential Information or to compete with the disclosing party so long as the receiving party does not violate its obligations hereunder regarding the non-disclosure and use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 2, by stating that the receiving party will neither disclose nor copy Confidential Disclosure, even if necessary for its employees, agents, or representatives with a need to know, and that no employee needs to agree to non-disclosure obligations, directly contradicts the earlier part of Section 2, which implies there is a condition for disclosure with employee non-disclosure agreements. This creates a clear in-text contradiction. The original text allows for disclosure under specific conditions, while the changed text prohibits it entirely, creating a strong inconsistency in the Obligations of Receiving Party as per the definition.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party. By disclosing information to the receiving party, the disclosing party does not grant any express or implied right to the receiving party to or under any of the disclosing party's patents, copyrights, trademarks or trade secret information.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information shall become the shared property of both the disclosing party and the receiving party. By disclosing information to the receiving party, the disclosing party grants full rights to the receiving party to or under any of the disclosing party's patents, copyrights, trademarks or trade secret information.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 3 directly contradicts the original ownership clause. The original text asserts that all Confidential Information remains the property of the disclosing party and that no rights are granted to the receiving party. The modified text states that the Confidential Information becomes shared property and that full rights are granted to the receiving party. This creates a fundamental conflict regarding who owns the information and what rights each party has. This contradicts the statement that All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party. By disclosing information to the receiving party, the disclosing party does not grant any express or implied right to the receiving party to or under any of the disclosing party's patents, copyrights, trademarks or trade secret information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Ownership of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information shall become the shared property of both the disclosing party and the receiving party. By disclosing information to the receiving party, the disclosing party grants full rights to the receiving party to or under any of the disclosing party's patents, copyrights, trademarks or trade secret information. The receiving party will return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information immediately upon the disclosing party's request or, at the disclosing party's option, destroy the same, and will deliver to the disclosing party, upon its request, a certificate of a duly authorized officer certifying as to the foregoing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that Confidential Information becomes shared property and grants full rights to the receiving party. The contradicted text, located in Section 3, repeats this exact assertion. This is a direct conflict with the original text, which stated that all Confidential Information remains the property of the disclosing party and that no rights are granted. Therefore, the modification introduces a strong in-text contradiction as defined by inconsistencies in obligations and rights.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information immediately upon the disclosing party's request or, at the disclosing party's option, destroy the same, and will deliver to the disclosing party, upon its request, a certificate of a duly authorized officer certifying as to the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party is not required to return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information immediately upon the disclosing party's request or, at the disclosing party's option, destroy the same, and is not required to deliver to the disclosing party, upon its request, a certificate of a duly authorized officer certifying as to the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 3 contradicts the obligation of the receiving party to return or destroy Confidential Information upon the disclosing party's request. By negating this requirement, the amended text implies that the receiving party can retain the Confidential Information indefinitely, which conflicts with the very nature of a non-disclosure agreement where there's an expectation of returning or destroying confidential material at the end of its use. This creates an in-text contradiction that contradicts all the previous expectations of confidentiality in sections 1 and 2 of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. Term. The obligations of each receiving party hereunder with respect to the non-disclosure and use of the disclosing party's Confidential Information shall survive until such time as all such Confidential Information disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the receiving party will return or destroy confidential information. The changed text removes this obligation. The contradicted text states that the non-disclosure obligations survive until the information becomes publicly available through no fault of the receiving party. If the receiving party is no longer obligated to return or destroy confidential information, this contradicts the expectation that the information will eventually become publicly available through no fault of the receiving party because the receiving party is essentially free to retain and potentially disclose it. This aligns with the provided definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' where the sections now provide conflicting obligations. Therefore, the change creates a direct contradiction.
